Muhammad’s most bitter adversaries, his uncle Ab‰ Lahab.

   May the hands of Ab‰ Lahab perish, and may he himself perish!
   His wealth and all that he has gained shall not avail him.

sections.text:
the surah opens with a vehement attack on one of Prophet
Muhammad’s most bitter adversaries, his uncle Ab‰ Lahab.

   May the hands of Ab‰ Lahab perish, and may he himself perish!
   His wealth and all that he has gained shall not avail him. (1–2)

Ab‰ Lahab was a wealthy and inﬂuential ﬁgure among the Arabs of
Makkah, but he soon came to grief. He was one of Muhammad’s
staunchest opponents, harboring intense resentment towards him,
and sparing no effort to slander him and his message.
    It is reported that one day the Prophet went to the hillock of al-
Safa, near the Ka¢bah in Makkah, and called the names of the
prominent clans of the Quraysh. A crowd, including his uncle Ab‰
Lahab, gathered around him, and he addressed them saying: “If I
were to tell you that enemy cavalry were in the valley preparing to
attack you, would you believe me?” They replied: “Indeed, we
would. We have never known you to lie.” The Prophet continued
to warn them of God’s severe punishment and to explain his mis-
sion, when Ab‰ Lahab interrupted angrily: “Damn you for the rest
of the day! Is this what you wanted to tell us?” It was shortly after-
wards, according to the reports, that this surah was revealed to
Muhammad. The reports go on to say that Ab‰ Lahab started to
throw stones at the Prophet.
    Of all Muhammad’s uncles, Ab‰ Lahab remained for the rest of

his life a most vicious opponent. This deep-seated animosity passed
on to his sons—those of whom who were married to daughters of
Muhammad decided to divorce them,—and to his wife. She was a
vicious, evil, and malicious woman who dedicated herself to slan-
dering Muhammad and smearing his name. She was a sister of Ab‰
Sufy¥n, Makkah’s most prominent leader and the closest it had to
a military general.
   The surah was revealed during the early days of Islam, and there
was ample opportunity for Ab‰ Lahab to retract his allegations and
join the Muslims, but he persisted and so the surah asserts that:

   He shall roast in a ﬂaming ﬁre, and so shall his wife, that carrier of
   ﬁrewood. She shall have a rope of entwined ﬁber round her
   neck. (3–5)

These powerfully graphic idiomatic expressions indicate that the
woman was zealous in her pursuit to revile the Prophet, and fanati-
cal in her hostility to Islam.
    Ab‰ Lahab, it seems, could never see Muhammad as anything
other than the poor orphan boy brought up by his grandfather,
¢Abd al-Mu~~alib, and his uncle Ab‰ >¥lib. He could not accept
that Muhammad could be chosen to receive divine revelation or
be commissioned for prophethood. He was blinded by his preju-
dice and jealousy all his life.
